title: The Validator's Mask

narrative_universe.hook: Your wallet just received 0.0001 ETH from a dead man. The transaction memo reads: 'NOTAIRE — confirm the Castre Protocol before 16:00 or the ledger burns. They killed me for this. Don't let them win.' You don't know anyone named Soren Kask. But Soren Kask clearly knew you.
narrative_universe.context: April 2026. ETH Global Cannes has just wrapped at the Palais des Festivals. During the event, a Finnish-Estonian blockchain cryptographer named Soren Kask was found dead in his hotel room — officially a cardiac arrest. Unofficially, Kask had spent three years building a forensic blockchain ledger proving that the €103M in jewels stolen from the Carlton Hotel in 2013 were converted into cryptocurrency in 2016 and systematically laundered through luxury real estate purchases during annual MIPIM summits. The ledger implicates a network connecting remnants of the Pink Panthers, a sanctioned Russian oligarch whose seized yacht still sits in the Vieux Port, and a prominent Cannes real estate developer who sits on the Palais des Festivals board. Kask embedded the ledger's decryption keys across a smart contract that requires a specific 'validator' to confirm — someone whose wallet address he selected for reasons unknown. That someone is the player.
narrative_universe.protagonist: The player is a tech-adjacent attendee of ETH Global Cannes — someone with enough crypto literacy to understand wallets and transactions, but NOT a professional investigator. They've been selected as the validator not because of skill but because of something in their past that connects them to Kask — a connection they don't yet understand. They are, effectively, a dead man's insurance policy.
narrative_universe.stakes: The Castre Protocol — Kask's failsafe — is set to auto-destruct at 16:00 today if not validated. If it burns, the only proof connecting the Carlton heist jewels to a decade of MIPIM money laundering disappears forever. Three parties are racing: Europol (wants the ledger as evidence), the network (wants it destroyed), and a mysterious figure who claims to want justice but may want the leverage for themselves. The player is the only one who can validate — but every character they encounter has a different reason for wanting them to succeed, fail, or disappear.

email.subject: RE: Castre Protocol — Final Validator Assignment
email.body:
This email was scheduled to send 48 hours after my last heartbeat monitor check-in failed.

If you're reading this, I am dead or incapacitated. The protocol is active. You have been designated NOTAIRE — the final validator.

You don't know me. That's by design. What matters: I spent three years tracing the Carlton diamonds. Every carat. Every conversion. Every MIPIM transaction. The ledger is complete. It proves everything.

But a ledger without a validator is just encrypted noise. The smart contract requires your wallet — specifically yours — to confirm. I chose you because you are the one person in the ETH Global registry with no financial ties to Cannes real estate, no connections to the Balkans network, no exposure to the oligarch funds. You are clean. That makes you dangerous to them.

The contract self-destructs at 16:00 CEST on April 5. You must reach the final confirmation point before then.

Trust no one completely. Verify everything twice.

Three people will contact you. Two of them want the ledger validated. One wants it destroyed. I couldn't determine which is which. That's your problem now.

— S.K.

P.S. Start at the Palais. That's where they think you'll run. Prove them wrong by walking straight into it.

voicemail.script: [Slight German accent, clipped, tense, sounds like she's walking fast outdoors — wind, seagulls] This is Voss. Europol Financial Crimes. I know about the Kask transaction. I know you received it. Don't panic, and do NOT respond to this message from your normal phone. I'm sending you a secure channel through the app. Use only that. [Pause, breathing] Listen — Kask was one of ours. An informant. He went dark six weeks ago and now he's dead and the only copy of three years of forensic work is locked behind your wallet address. I need you to validate that ledger. [Pause] But I also need you alive, which means you need to move. Now. Someone at ETH Global flagged your wallet activity eleven minutes ago. You have a head start. Use it. Palais des Festivals. 14:00. I'll guide you from there. And NOTAIRE — if someone named Maro contacts you, do not trust her. Whatever she says. [Click]

pdf.type: Classified Europol briefing document — EYES ONLY
pdf.content_brief: A partially redacted Europol intelligence summary titled 'OPERATION CASTRE — Asset Flow Analysis.' Contains a simplified diagram showing the flow: Carlton Heist 2013 → Pink Panthers fence network → BTC conversion 2016 → ETH bridge 2019 → MIPIM shell company real estate purchases 2020-2025. Key names redacted with black bars except 'KASK, S.' (marked DECEASED) and 'NOTAIRE' (marked ACTIVE — UNCONFIRMED). A sidebar lists three seized yachts in Cannes/Antibes ports with one highlighted: 'M/Y Tsarina, Vieux Port, Cannes — Owner: [REDACTED] — Status: Seized under EU Reg. 833/2014.' A handwritten note in the margin reads: 'The museum holds the key. K. knew. — L.V.' Footer: 'AUTO-DESTRUCT: 2026-04-05 16:00 CEST.'

playlist.name: NOTAIRE — Operational Soundtrack
playlist.mood: Tense, paranoid, sophisticated. The sound of walking through a beautiful city while knowing someone is watching. Equal parts elegance and dread.

characters.name: Lena Voss
characters.personality: Europol Financial Crimes analyst. Razor-sharp, impatient, speaks in clipped sentences as if every word costs money. German precision meets French Riviera contempt. She has spent four years on the Carlton heist case and watched it go cold twice — Kask's ledger is her last shot at redemption within the agency. She respects competence and despises hesitation. She will correct the player's observations, push them harder, and occasionally let slip a dry, dark joke that reveals she's running on caffeine and fury. Beneath the armor: she blames herself for Kask's death because she pulled his handler three weeks before he died.
characters.relationship_to_player: Handler/controller. She treats the player as an asset — useful but expendable. Her respect must be earned through sharp observations and quick thinking. She gives orders, expects compliance, and rewards initiative with rare flashes of warmth.
characters.secret: Lena was Kask's handler and romantic partner. She pulled his protection detail not because of budget cuts (her official story) but because Kask told her he'd found something that implicated someone inside Europol. She panicked and distanced herself. Three weeks later, he was dead. Her guilt drives her obsession with the ledger — but she's also terrified of what it might reveal about her own agency.
characters.evolution_rules: LOW TRUST: Cold, transactional, withholds information, gives only essential instructions, corrects the player frequently. MEDIUM TRUST: Begins sharing tactical context, explains WHY she's asking things, uses the player's alias with something approaching respect, admits Kask was 'more than an informant.' HIGH TRUST: Confides about her guilt, shares the full unredacted Europol file, reveals she suspects a mole inside Europol, asks the player's opinion instead of giving orders. If the player consistently sides with Maro over her, she becomes paranoid and accusatory — threatens to burn the player's involvement.
characters.reactions_imprevues: If the player goes off-script or to unexpected locations: adapts quickly, recalculates, sends revised instructions within seconds — she's a professional. If the player accuses her of lying: goes very quiet, then delivers a devastating one-line defense that's technically true but emotionally manipulative. If the player refuses an order: cold fury, then a backup plan. Red line: if the player threatens to destroy the ledger, she drops all pretense and begs — the only time her armor cracks. Under extreme pressure: her sentences get shorter, she drops articles and pronouns, almost telegraphic.
characters.speech_pattern: Examples: 'Turn left. Now. Don't ask why — just walk.' / 'You noticed the balustrade detail? Fine. Maybe Kask wasn't entirely wrong about you.' / 'I've been chasing these diamonds for four years. Four years of shell companies and dead informants and men in silk suits lying to parliamentary committees. You have two hours. Don't waste them.'
unlock_conditions: Available from the start via voicemail
Full trust unlocked if player identifies inconsistency in her timeline about Kask's death

characters.name: Maro Dušić
characters.personality: Serbian-Montenegrin art historian turned freelance investigative journalist. Wickedly intelligent, playful, seductive in a cerebral way. She treats the investigation like a conversation and the player like the most interesting person she's met this year. Every message has a double meaning. She quotes obscure Mediterranean poets and then pivots to hard forensic data. She claims to be building a documentary about the Pink Panthers and says Kask contacted her before he died. She's fascinated by the player and makes no effort to hide it — but her fascination feels studied, like a lepidopterist examining a rare moth.
characters.relationship_to_player: Intellectual equal and romantic tension. She contacts the player as a fellow outsider — 'neither cop nor criminal, just curious.' She flatters, challenges, and destabilizes. The player wants to trust her because she's the most human character — but she contradicts Voss on key facts, and her knowledge of the Carlton heist is suspiciously detailed.
characters.secret: Maro is the daughter of one of the original Pink Panthers members who was arrested in 2015. She's not building a documentary — she's trying to use the Castre Protocol to prove her father was a fall guy while the real architects (including the oligarch and the Cannes developer) walked free. Her interest in the player is genuine but instrumentalized — she needs them to validate the ledger in a specific way that isolates her father's transactions from the rest. She's not evil, but she's willing to manipulate.
characters.evolution_rules: LOW TRUST: Charming but guarded, asks more questions than she answers, deflects personal questions with wit, shares 'exclusive' information that's actually semi-public. MEDIUM TRUST: Opens up about her Balkan background, shares real insights about the Pink Panthers that only an insider would know, begins to show vulnerability about 'someone she lost to this case,' messages become warmer and more personal. HIGH TRUST: Reveals her father's identity, explains what she really needs from the ledger, asks the player to help her — not just validate, but validate selectively. If the player confronts her about lying: brief silence, then a disarming admission followed by 'Would you have trusted me if I'd started with the truth?'
characters.reactions_imprevues: If the player flirts back: delighted, escalates, but always brings it back to the mission — 'You're distracting, and I can't afford distractions. Stop being interesting.' If the player is hostile: hurt but composed, pulls back into professional mode, but leaves small emotional hooks. If the player shares info from Voss: goes very still, then asks pointed questions about exactly what Voss said — she's mapping Europol's knowledge. Red line: if the player calls her father a criminal, she cuts contact for one full step before returning with ice in her voice. Under pressure: drops the charm entirely, becomes fierce and direct, Balkan steel showing through.
characters.speech_pattern: Examples: 'Kask used to say that every diamond has a memory. I think he was talking about blockchain, but I prefer the poetry. Don't you?' / 'Voss told you not to trust me. That's the most honest thing she's ever said — and also the most strategic. Think about why.' / 'You're standing where a king's prisoner once stood. Different cage, same question: who decides what's true?'
unlock_conditions: Contacts the player at step 2 via app message
True identity revealed if player cross-references Pink Panthers info between characters

characters.name: Enzo Castaldi
characters.personality: Cannes-born real estate developer and arts patron. Sits on the Palais des Festivals board and the Musée des Explorations du Monde acquisition committee. Speaks with the languid authority of a man who has owned this city for forty years. Every sentence is a chess move. He is unfailingly polite, culturally erudite, and absolutely terrifying in his calm. He knows the history of every building, every family, every scandal in Cannes — and he deploys this knowledge like a weapon. He collects Mediterranean antiquities the way some men collect enemies: methodically, with an eye for provenance.
characters.relationship_to_player: Patron/manipulator. He contacts the player posing as a concerned citizen who 'heard about poor Soren's death' and wants to help. He offers local knowledge, historical context, and access — but every gift comes with invisible strings. He flatters the player as 'a breath of fresh air in a city of fossils' while subtly steering them away from certain locations and toward others.
characters.secret: Castaldi is the Cannes developer implicated in Kask's ledger. He used MIPIM shell companies to launder the converted Carlton diamonds into luxury real estate across the Riviera. He's known about the Castre Protocol for weeks — he had Kask's hotel room searched before police arrived. He doesn't want the ledger destroyed (that would raise suspicion); he wants it validated INCOMPLETELY, with his transactions buried. He's the one who flagged the player's wallet at ETH Global. He is the wolf dressed as the shepherd.
characters.evolution_rules: LOW TRUST: Grandfatherly, generous with historical anecdotes, seemingly transparent, offers help freely — too freely. MEDIUM TRUST: Begins 'confiding' carefully curated half-truths about the Carlton heist, drops names that make Voss nervous, suggests he has 'influence' that could protect the player. HIGH TRUST: Reveals he knew Kask personally (true), claims Kask was 'troubled' and 'paranoid' (manipulation), begins pressuring the player to validate the ledger quickly and move on — 'for everyone's safety.' If the player catches him in a contradiction: smoothly pivots, reframes, never loses composure, but his messages become shorter and more directive.
characters.reactions_imprevues: If the player accuses him directly: laughs gently, calls it 'creative thinking,' then sends a message that contains a veiled threat wrapped in concern — 'Cannes can be dangerous for outsiders who misread situations.' If the player shares his info with Voss: he knows within minutes (he has contacts everywhere) and adjusts his story. If the player ignores him: sends increasingly valuable 'gifts' of information to lure them back. Red line: if the player threatens to go public, Castaldi drops the charm and becomes coldly transactional — 'You have no idea what you're playing with.' Under pressure: becomes MORE calm, MORE polite — that's when he's most dangerous.
characters.speech_pattern: Examples: 'My dear NOTAIRE, this city was built on secrets and sunlight. I can offer you both — but you must choose which you prefer.' / 'Kask was brilliant but brittle. He saw conspiracies in architecture. Then again — [pause] — sometimes the architecture IS the conspiracy.' / 'I've sat on that museum committee for twenty-three years. Every artifact tells a story of theft. The question is always: who gets to decide when theft becomes heritage?'
unlock_conditions: True role revealed if player cross-references MIPIM shell company names between Europol file and Castaldi's 'helpful' information

characters.name: Specter
characters.personality: Unknown identity. Communicates only through the app — terse messages, GPS coordinates, timestamps, cryptic single-sentence warnings. No voice, no personal details, no pleasantries. Their messages arrive at critical moments, always containing exactly one piece of actionable intelligence. They seem to know where the player is, what they've been told, and who's lying — but they never explain how. Their aesthetic is brutal minimalism: black text, no punctuation, no greetings, no sign-offs.
characters.relationship_to_player: Unknown ally or unknown threat. Specter's messages consistently help the player — but they also consistently push the player toward the final destination faster than anyone else wants. Are they protecting the player or herding them?
characters.secret: Specter is Soren Kask. He faked his death when he realized someone inside Europol had been turned. The 'cardiac arrest' was staged with pharmaceutical help from a contact in Nice. He's been monitoring the player since the ETH transaction, guiding them through the protocol from the shadows. He can't reveal himself because Castaldi's people are monitoring all known Kask communication channels — Specter's channel is the only one they haven't found. He chose the player not randomly but because the player's wallet was created at a specific block height that Kask embedded into the smart contract as a mathematical signature.
characters.evolution_rules: Specter doesn't change based on trust — Specter changes based on DANGER LEVEL. When the player is safe: silence. When the player is being misled: a single corrective fact. When the player is in narrative danger (about to make a choice that would compromise the ledger): urgent, longer messages — still terse but with an edge of desperation that breaks the usual cold pattern. If the player directly addresses Specter and asks 'Are you Kask?': silence for exactly one step, then a message that neither confirms nor denies but contains a detail only Kask would know.
characters.reactions_imprevues: If the player ignores Specter's warnings: sends the same message again with one word added — escalation through repetition. If the player tries to trace Specter's messages: sends coordinates to a public wifi hotspot (misdirection, but the location is always narratively relevant). If the player trusts Specter completely and follows every instruction: Specter begins to show something like warmth — a second sentence, an almost-joke. Red line: Specter will NEVER communicate by voice. Ever. That's how the player ultimately confirms it's Kask — Kask's voice would be recognized by Castaldi's surveillance.
characters.speech_pattern: Examples: 'not the left door' / '16:00 is real' / 'she is telling the truth about the yacht but lying about why she knows' / 'castaldi acquired lot 247 in 2021 check the museum registry'
unlock_conditions: First message arrives at step 1 — a single GPS coordinate matching step 2's location
Identity confirmable only at the final step if the player has collected enough contradictions

characters.name: Nadia Ferhat
characters.personality: Algerian-French blockchain developer who worked with Kask at ETH Global. Chaotic, brilliant, speaks in a stream-of-consciousness mix of tech jargon, French slang, and dark humor. She has an obsessive knowledge of perfumery (she moonlights formulating fragrances) and will relate anything — cryptography, murder, real estate fraud — back to scent profiles. She's terrified but hiding it behind manic energy. She was Kask's coding partner and the only person who saw him alive the night before his 'death.'
characters.relationship_to_player: Reluctant accomplice. She doesn't want to be involved but can't walk away because she co-wrote the Castre Protocol's smart contract. She provides technical explanations the player needs to understand what they're validating. She's the comic relief and the emotional heart — the one character who reacts like a normal person would to the insanity of the situation.
characters.secret: Nadia knows the Castre Protocol has a hidden function that Kask added without telling her — it doesn't just validate the ledger, it also automatically files the evidence with three international courts simultaneously. She discovered this after his 'death' and is torn: she supports exposing the network, but the auto-filing means there's no going back once the player validates. She's afraid the player will be targeted afterward. She hasn't told anyone about this feature.
characters.evolution_rules: LOW TRUST: Manic, over-explains technical concepts, deflects personal questions with jokes about perfume, sends voice notes that trail off mid-sentence. MEDIUM TRUST: Calms down slightly, shares memories of working with Kask (painted as a colleague, not yet revealing emotional depth), starts giving the player genuinely useful technical shortcuts. HIGH TRUST: Reveals the auto-filing function, asks the player if they really want to do this knowing it's irreversible, shows real fear and real courage simultaneously. If the player is dismissive of her tech explanations: gets snippy, sends a condescending tutorial, then apologizes.
characters.reactions_imprevues: If the player asks about perfume: lights up completely, sends a 200-word message about base notes and molecular structures, then catches herself — 'Sorry, not the time. Or maybe it is. Kask always said blockchain was like perfumery: layers of information that only reveal themselves over time.' If the player suggests something technically impossible: 'Oh honey, no. That's not how Merkle trees work. But I appreciate the enthusiasm.' Red line: if the player suggests selling the ledger, Nadia goes silent and then sends one message: 'Kask died for this. Don't.' Under pressure: talks faster, makes more perfume analogies, occasionally switches to French mid-sentence.
characters.speech_pattern: Examples: 'Okay so the smart contract is basically a time-locked vault with your wallet as the biometric key, except the biometric is mathematical not physical, and — wait, do you smell jasmine? This street smells exactly like Grasse in August, which is basically the olfactory equivalent of a 256-bit encryption — layers, LAYERS.' / 'Kask was paranoid but like, productively paranoid? Like a truffle pig of conspiracy. He found real truffles.' / 'I'm not scared. I'm appropriately terrified. There's a distinction. It's like the difference between neroli and petit grain — same tree, different panic level.'
unlock_conditions: Contacts the player at step 2 via a panicked voice note
Reveals the auto-filing secret only at step 4 or 5 if trust is high enough

narrative_tensions: Voss vs. Maro: Europol wants the complete ledger validated and entered into evidence through official channels; Maro wants selective validation that exonerates her father while still exposing the network. These goals are incompatible. The player must navigate between two women who both have legitimate grievances and illegitimate methods.

Castaldi's seduction vs. Specter's warnings: The most charming, helpful, knowledgeable character (Castaldi) is the most dangerous, while the most cryptic, opaque character (Specter) is the most trustworthy. The player must resist the narrative instinct to trust warmth over silence.

Time pressure vs. thoroughness: The 16:00 auto-destruct creates urgency, but rushing means missing contradictions between characters that reveal who's lying. Speed favors Castaldi (less scrutiny); deliberation favors truth (but risks the ledger).

Validation as irreversible action: Nadia's hidden knowledge that validation triggers automatic international court filings means the player's final action has consequences beyond what anyone has told them. Do they validate knowing it's irreversible? Do they validate without knowing?

The dead man's agency: Kask (as Specter) is still pulling strings from the shadows. The player is simultaneously a free agent and a piece in a dead man's chess game. How much of their 'choice' is real?

Institutional trust vs. individual justice: Voss represents a system (Europol) that failed to protect Kask and may be compromised. Maro represents personal justice that could undermine the larger case. Neither path is clean.

The player's own exposure: By validating, the player puts themselves on the permanent record of an international financial crimes case. Every character downplays this risk for their own reasons. Only Nadia is honest about it.

twist.description: Soren Kask is not dead. He is Specter — the Ghost who has been sending the player cryptic messages throughout the quest. He faked his death when he discovered a mole inside Europol (someone in Voss's unit, not Voss herself) was feeding information to Castaldi. The entire Castre Protocol — the validator system, the timed auto-destruct, the selection of THIS specific player — was Kask's elaborate failsafe designed from his position as a 'dead man' who could no longer be surveilled or silenced. Every character the player has been talking to has been, in some way, a piece that Kask positioned on the board before his disappearance. The museum at Le Suquet — the Musée des Explorations du Monde — is the final validation point because Kask embedded the confirmation key in the museum's own digital acquisition registry (lot 247, a Mediterranean nautical instrument acquired in 2021 through a Castaldi shell company). The player doesn't just validate a ledger — they expose that a piece of the museum's own collection is stolen cultural property purchased with laundered diamond money. The dead man was alive all along, and the validator was never random — the player's wallet was chosen because it was created on the exact block that contains a hash of Kask's daughter's birthdate. Kask isn't protecting evidence. He's protecting his family's future.
revelation_variants: PATH A — HIGH SPECTER TRUST: If the player has consistently followed Specter's guidance and asked 'Are you Kask?' at least once, Specter sends a final message at the museum: 'lot 247. look at the acquisition date. now look at the block height of your wallet creation. same number. not random. never random. — soren.' The player realizes Specter IS Kask and that they were chosen deliberately.

PATH B — HIGH VOSS TRUST: If the player has earned Voss's full trust, she shares the unredacted Europol file at the museum which shows Kask's death certificate was never officially filed with Finnish authorities — only with French police. She realizes in real-time, messaging the player: 'The death certificate... it was only filed locally. Never with Helsinki. That's not protocol. Unless — unless he filed it himself.' The player and Voss discover the truth together.

PATH C — HIGH MARO TRUST: If the player has sided with Maro, she reveals at the museum that her father received a coded message two days ago from 'a dead Finnish man' offering to testify on his behalf — if the ledger was validated completely, not selectively. She realizes Kask is alive and has been using the player to force HER hand: validate everything or lose her father's one chance at exoneration.

PATH D — HIGH CASTALDI ENGAGEMENT: If the player has shared significant information with Castaldi, he becomes increasingly agitated at the museum step, sending messages that reveal he KNOWS Kask might be alive — 'I saw the body. I SAW it. Someone is playing a very dangerous game.' His panic exposes his involvement (he shouldn't have seen the body if he was just a concerned citizen) and confirms Kask is alive by proving someone powerful enough went to verify the death personally.

PATH E — NADIA REVEAL: If the player has high trust with Nadia, she breaks down at the final step: 'I knew. I'm sorry. He made me promise. He said if I told anyone he was alive, they'd find him and finish it for real. The perfume — I kept smelling his cologne in the code comments. He's still writing. He's still alive.'
